items.title: Image classifier with 80/10/10 split
items.scenario: A team has 100,000 labeled photos. They train on 80,000, tune augmentation and learning rate on 10,000 validation images, and report final accuracy once on 10,000 test images.
items.explanation: Validation supports many experiments; the test set stays untouched until the final model is chosen — a standard three-way workflow.

items.title: Fraud model with temporal split
items.scenario: Transactions from Jan–Sep form the train set, Oct is validation for threshold tuning, Nov is the locked test month.
items.explanation: Random shuffling would leak future fraud patterns into training. Temporal splits mirror production: the model scores transactions it has never seen from later months.

items.title: Medical triage with patient-level hold-out
items.scenario: Hospital records include multiple visits per patient. Splits assign entire patients to train, validation, or test — never splitting visits from the same patient across sets.
items.explanation: Visit-level random splits leak patient identity across partitions and inflate validation/test scores. Group hold-out keeps rows truly unseen at the patient level.

items.title: Small tabular dataset — thin validation slice
items.scenario: A startup has 800 labeled rows. They use 560 train, 120 validation, 120 test and notice high variance in validation scores across random seeds.
items.explanation: Small validation sets are noisy for model selection; teams often add cross-validation on train+val while keeping a modest locked test set.

items.title: Hyperparameter grid search on validation only
items.scenario: An engineer tries 50 random forest depths, picking the depth with best validation F1, then retrains on train+validation and evaluates once on test.
items.explanation: All 50 comparisons use validation — not test. The test score is the unbiased estimate after choices are finalized.

items.title: Stratified split for rare disease detection
items.scenario: Only 2% of screening images are positive. Stratified splitting ensures each partition has roughly 2% positives.
items.explanation: Without stratification, a random split might put almost no positives in validation or test, making metrics meaningless and model selection unreliable.
